statementLines: Consider the following types of electromagnetic radiations:
I. X-rays
II. Microwaves
III. Gamma rays
IV. Radio waves
How many of the above radiations are considered as examples of Ionizing radiation?
questions.questionText: Consider the following types of electromagnetic radiations: I. X-rays II. Microwaves III. Gamma rays IV. Radio waves How many of the above radiations are considered as examples of Ionizing radiation?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **There are two types of radiation: non Ionizing radiation and Ionizing radiation. Non-ionizing radiation is a lower energy radiation, which means there is a lack of adequate energy to completely remove an electron from an atom or molecule (a process called ionization). Thus, instead of producing charged ions while passing through the matter or organisms, they make those molecules vibrate (a process called excitation), ultimately producing heat. Some examples of it are: visible light,**radio waves, microwave**etc. Thus**option II, and IV is **incorrect.**Ionizing radiation is a type of radiation where due to a large amount of energy electrons can be detached from atoms or molecules when interacting with matter as well as organisms. This leads to the production of ions (electrically charged atoms or molecules) and exposure to it can cause cell damage to living tissue and even organ damage. Example:**Ultra violet, X-ray, Gamma ray. Thus Option I and III is correct.**

statementLines: With reference to the National Clean Air Programme (NCAP), consider the following statements:
I. Under the programme the target has been set to achieve reduction in PM10 level up to 40% or achievement of national ambient air quality standards by 2025-26 from the levels of 2019-20.
II. The PRANA portal is a portal for managing and monitoring the implementation of programme.
III. Swachh Vayu Survekshan is an annual survey conducted by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate
Change under NCAP.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: With reference to the National Clean Air Programme (NCAP), consider the following statements: I. Under the programme the target has been set to achieve reduction in PM10 level up to 40% or achievement of national ambient air quality standards by 2025-26 from the levels of 2019-20. II. The PRANA portal is a portal for managing and monitoring the implementation of programme. III. Swachh Vayu Survekshan is an annual survey conducted by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change under NCAP. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. Air pollution is no longer seen as a seasonal or city-specific problem in India. It is now treated as a structural environmental challenge with serious public health costs. The National Clean Air Programme was launched by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change in January 2019 to improve air quality in 130 (non-attainment cities and Million Plus Cities) in 24 States/UTs. It aims to bring uniformity, accountability and long-term planning into air quality management across Indian cities. Its design rests on clear targets, digital monitoring and competitive performance assessment.**

Statement I is correct**. The National Clean Air Programme was initially framed with a 20–30% reduction target for PM2.5 and PM10 levels as compared to 2017 levels by 2024. This target was later**revised upward**. The current**target is **to achieve a reduction in PM10 level up to 40% or achievement of national ambient air quality standards (60 **µg/m3) by **2025-26** from the levels of **2019-20**. **

Statement II is correct**. “**PRANA**” stands for Portal for Regulation of Air-Pollution in Non-Attainment Cities. It is**a **portal for managing and monitoring the implementation of the National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)**. It supports tracking the physical as well as financial status of city air action plan implementation and disseminates information on air quality management efforts under NCAP to the public.**

Statement III is correct**.**Swachh Vayu Survekshan**is an**annual survey**conducted by the**Ministry of Environment, **Forest and Climate Change**under the framework of NCAP. It evaluates cities on multiple parameters such as dust control, waste management, emission control and public awareness. The 2025 edition, in which**Indore topped the **million-plus cities category**, reinforces the use of competition as a governance tool to improve air quality outcomes.

statementLines: With reference to the institutional framework of Disaster Management in India under the Disaster Management
Act, 2005, consider the following pairs:
Body Chairperson
I. National Disaster Management Authority Prime Minister
II. National Crisis Management Committee Cabinet Secretary
III. National Executive Committee Union Home Minister
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.questionText: With reference to the institutional framework of Disaster Management in India under the Disaster Management Act, 2005, consider the following pairs: Body Chairperson I. National Disaster Management Authority Prime Minister II. National Crisis Management Committee Cabinet Secretary III. National Executive Committee Union Home Minister How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**. Disaster management in India functions through a clearly defined institutional hierarchy created under the Disaster Management Act, 2005. Each national-level body has a specific mandate, and the person heading the body reflects its role in policy formulation, coordination or operational monitoring.**Pair I is correct**. The**National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA)**is the apex policy-making body for disaster management in India. It is**chaired by the Prime Minister**, which underlines the national importance of disaster risk reduction and preparedness. The NDMA is responsible for laying down policies, guidelines and plans for managing all types of disasters, whether natural or man-made.**Pair II is correct**. The**National Crisis Management Committee (NCMC)**is**chaired by the Cabinet Secretary**, the senior-most civil servant in the Government of India. The NCMC plays a crucial role during crisis situations by overseeing command, control and coordination of disaster response. It issues directions to ministries and departments for immediate and time-bound action. It has been given legal status recently under section 8A of Disaster Management Act, 2005, post 2025 amendment to the principal act.**Pair III is incorrect**. The**National Executive Committee (NEC)**is**not chaired by the Union Home Minister**. Instead, it is**chaired by the Secretary to the Government of India in charge of the Ministry or Department of the Central **Government having administrative control of the disaster management (Union Home Secretary)**. The NEC functions as the coordinating and monitoring body for disaster management and assists the NDMA in implementing policies and national plans. It has been established under section 10(1) of Disaster Management Act, 2005.

consider the following pairs:
Type of Drought Meaning
I. Meteorological Drought A period during which the stream flows are inadequate to supply established use of water under a given water management system.
II. Agricultural Drought It occurs when available soil moisture is inadequate for healthy crop growth and cause extreme stress and wilting.
III. Hydrological Drought A situation when the seasonal rainfall received over the area is less than 75% of its long term average value.
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.questionText: In the context of India, droughts over an area for a time period are classified into different types, in this context consider the following pairs: Type of Drought Meaning I. Meteorological Drought A period during which the stream flows are inadequate to supply established use of water under a given water management system. II. Agricultural Drought It occurs when available soil moisture is inadequate for healthy crop growth and cause extreme stress and wilting. III. Hydrological Drought A situation when the seasonal rainfall received over the area is less than 75% of its long term average value. How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Drought is a temporary reduction in water or moisture availability significantly below the normal or expected amount for a specific period. This condition occurs either due to inadequacy of rainfall, or lack of irrigation facilities.**Pair I is incorrect. Meteorological Drought refers to lack of precipitation. **It describes a situation where there is a reduction in rainfall for a specific period below a specific amount. It is a condition when an area receives less than the specified rainfall. According to India Meteorological Department,**meteorological drought**over an area**is defined as a situation when **the seasonal rainfall received over the area is less than 75% of its long term average value.**Hydrological Drought refers to low levels of water in reservoirs.**Pair II is correct. Agricultural Drought refers to lack of moisture in the soil where crops grow**. It occurs when available soil moisture is inadequate for healthy crop growth and causes extreme stress and wilting.**Pair III is incorrect. Hydrological Drought refers to low levels of water in reservoirs**. Hydrological Drought involves a reduction in water resources (stream flow, lake level, ground water, underground aquifers) below a specified level for a given period of time. It means low water supply in rivers, lakes, aquifers, and other reservoirs.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **

Statement 1 is correct: Gold mining is poisoning Amazon forests with mercury.**To separate the gold, miners mix liquid mercury into the sediment, which forms a coating around the gold.**

Statement 2 is correct: Fly ash generated by TPPs is one of the ways mercury is released into the environment**. Fly ash is usually disposed of in ash ponds in the form of ash slurry, forming an aquatic ecosystem of its own. Coal-based TPPs are the major source of mercury-the ninth most toxic element found on earth — emissions into the environment.**Mercury contributed over 80 per cent of emissions released by the TPPs**, according to 2016 estimates by Delhi-based think-tank Centre for Science and Environment.**

Statement 3 is incorrect: **While high levels of mercury exposure can be harmful, there are established safe levels of exposure to mercury for various scenarios**. As per different reports currently, mercury vapour concentrations **greater than 0.01 mg/m3 are considered unsafe.**.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
statementLines: Consider the following air pollutants:
I. Tropospheric Ozone
II. Smog
III. Sulphur dioxide
How many of the above are primarily secondary pollutants?
questions.questionText: Consider the following air pollutants: I. Tropospheric Ozone II. Smog III. Sulphur dioxide How many of the above are primarily secondary pollutants?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Air pollution refers to the presence of harmful substances in the atmosphere in concentrations that can adversely affect human health, ecosystems, materials, and climate. Based on their mode of origin,**air pollutants are broadly **classified into primary pollutants and secondary pollutants**:**Primary pollutants **are those that are**emitted directly into the atmosphere**from identifiable sources such as vehicles, industries, power plants, biomass burning, and natural events (e.g., volcanic eruptions). Examples include**sulphur dioxide, **particulates, carbon dioxide and carbon monoxide.**Secondary pollutants are not released directly from sources. **Instead, they are**formed in the atmosphere through **physical and chemical reactions involving primary pollutants**, often under the influence of sunlight, oxygen, moisture, and oxidizing agents. These pollutants are responsible for phenomena such as**smog, acid rain, and ground
- **level ozone formation. ** Option I is correct: Tropospheric Ozone: Secondary pollutant **Ozone present in the troposphere (ground-level ozone) is not emitted directly from any source. It is**formed when **nitrogen oxides**(NOₓ)**and volatile organic compounds**(VOCs)**react in the presence of sunlight.**Ground-level ozone is a major component of urban air pollution and causes respiratory problems and damage to vegetation. Hence,**it is a secondary pollutant. ** Formation of Tropospheric Ozone ** Option II is correct: Smog: Secondary pollutant **Smog is a complex**mixture of pollutants**formed due to atmospheric reactions**involving primary pollutants **such as nitrogen oxides, and hydrocarbons triggered by sunlight**. It is formed mostly in urban areas, especially in stagnant air. Since smog forms after chemical transformation in the atmosphere, it is**classified as a secondary pollutant. ** Option III is incorrect: Sulphur dioxide (SO₂**): Primary pollutant** Sulphur dioxide is **directly released into the atmosphere** from sources such as coal-based thermal power plants, oil refineries, metal smelting industries, and volcanic eruptions. It does not require atmospheric reactions for its formation and **therefore is a primary pollutant**. However, SO₂ can later participate in reactions to form secondary pollutants like sulphuric acid aerosols.**

statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding India’s greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions:
I. Between 1850 and 2019, India’s share of global cumulative greenhouse gas emissions has been less than 5 percent.
II. India’s emission intensity of its GDP annually has consistently increased during the period from 2005 till 2020.

questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding India’s greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions: I. Between 1850 and 2019, India’s share of global cumulative greenhouse gas emissions has been less than 5 percent. II. India’s emission intensity of its GDP annually has consistently increased during the period from 2005 till 2020. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct:**India’s contribution to cumulative global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions**since the beginning of the industrial era has**remained below 5 percent (approximately 4%)**, despite India being home to around**17% of the world’s population**between 1850 and 2019**. This reflects India’s late industrialisation and comparatively lower per-capita emissions over a long historical period, a key argument used by India in international climate negotiations to emphasise the principle of**common but **differentiated responsibilities**(CBDR).**

Statement II is incorrect: India’s emission intensity of GDP **(GHG emissions per unit of GDP)** has shown a declining **trend, not an increasing one, during the period 2005–2020. **According to**India’s Fourth Biennial Update Report**submitted to the UNFCCC,**India reduced the emission intensity **of its GDP by about 36% between 2005 and 2020.** This decline has been driven by improved energy efficiency, expansion of renewable energy, and structural changes in the economy.

They have a strong tendency to bioaccumulate and biomagnify in food chains and even at very low concentrations, can cause serious health effects including cancer, reproductive disorders, and immune system impairment. Which one of the following persistent organic pollutants is best described in the above passage?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The**passage clearly describes Dioxins**, which are a**group of highly toxic, persistent organic pollutants (POPs).**Dioxins are**mainly by-products of industrial processes**but can also result from natural processes, such as volcanic eruptions and forest fires. They are**unintended or unwanted by-products of many manufacturing processes**including**smelting, chlorine **bleaching of paper pulp, waste incineration, **and the manufacture of some herbicides and pesticides. They are**highly persistent, lipophilic**, and therefore**bioaccumulate i**n fatty tissues and**biomagnify along food **chains**, leading to higher concentrations at higher trophic levels. Even at very low concentrations,**dioxins can cause severe health impacts**, including:**Carcinogenic effects, **reproductive and developmental disorders and endocrine and immune system impairment.**Under the**Stockholm Convention**, dioxins are listed in**Annex C,**which focuses on**unintentionally produced POPs**, with the objective of their reduction and, where feasible, elimination.**

Option a is incorrect: Aldrin**: An organochlorine insecticide that was**intentionally produced**and used in agriculture; later banned due to toxicity. It is listed under**Annex A**of the Stockholm Convention for elimination.**

Option b is incorrect: DDT: **It is also an**intentionally manufactured pesticide**, historically used for malaria control; restricted but not an unintentional by-product. It is listed under**Annex B**of the Stockholm Convention with restricted use, notably for vector control (malaria).**

Option d is incorrect: Polychlorinated Biphenyls (PCBs): **Intentionally manufactured industrial chemicals**used earlier as coolants and insulating fluids; although highly toxic and persistent, they are not unintentional by-products. It is listed under**Annex A**of the Stockholm Convention for elimination. Knowledge Base:**Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs):**POPs**are a group of toxic organic chemicals that possess the following key characteristics: -**Persistence**: They resist environmental degradation and remain in the environment for long periods. -**Bioaccumulation**: They accumulate in the fatty tissues of living organisms. -**Biomagnification**: Their concentration increases at successive trophic levels of the food chain. -**Long-range transport**: They can travel long distances through air and water, affecting regions far from their source. -**High toxicity**: Even at low concentrations, they can cause serious health effects such as cancer, endocrine disruption, reproductive and developmental disorders, and immune suppression. - Examples include**DDT, Aldrin, PCBs, Dioxins, Furans, and Hexachlorobenzene**.**Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants **The**Stockholm Convention**is a**global legally binding treaty**adopted in**2001**and entered into force in**2004**. It aims to**protect human health and the environment**from POPs**by controlling their production, use, trade, release, and disposal.**India is a Party**to the Convention.**Annexes under the Convention: **-**Annex A (Elimination):**POPs to be eliminated completely (e.g., Aldrin, Chlordane, PCBs). -**Annex B (Restriction):**POPs with restricted use (e.g.,**DDT**for disease vector control). -**Annex C (Unintentional Production):**POPs formed as**by-products**, such as**Dioxins and Furans**, where the goal is**reduction and, where feasible, elimination** of releases.

statementLines: With reference to water quality assessment, the Most Probable Number (MPN) parameter is primarily used to indicate:
questions.questionText: With reference to water quality assessment, the Most Probable Number (MPN) parameter is primarily used to indicate:

options.a: The presence of toxic heavy metals such as lead and mercury in water
options.b: The level of dissolved oxygen available for aquatic organisms
options.c: The total quantity of dissolved salts and solids in water
options.d: The extent of coliform (faecal) bacterial contamination in water

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **The**Most Probable Number**(MPN) is a**statistical microbiological technique used in water quality assessment **to**detect and enumerate coliform bacteria, including E. coli, in a water sample.**Water polluted with organic wastes such as sewage or sludge typically supports a high population of these bacteria, resulting in elevated MPN values. Coliform bacteria are**naturally present in the human intestinal tract**and are not necessarily harmful by themselves. However,**their presence in water serves as a reliable indicator of faecal contamination.**Consequently,**a high MPN value reflects poor sanitary quality**and an increased likelihood of the presence of pathogenic microorganisms. The**safe limit of faecal coliform bacteria**set by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) is**2,500 MPN (Most **Probable Number)/100ml. ** MPN testing is especially important in the assessment of drinking water safety and public health, as it reflects the sanitary condition of water sources, rather than their chemical or physical quality.

statementLines: Which one of the following introduced the concept of Extended Producer Responsibility for the first time for the collection of e-waste in India?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following introduced the concept of Extended Producer Responsibility for the first time for the collection of e-waste in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **To streamline e-waste management, the government notified Electronic Waste Rules in 2011, based on the concept **of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR).**EPR makes manufacturers of electronic products responsible for the end-of-life management of their products, including setting up collection centers. By shifting the burden of EPR to manufacturers, the EPR framework, in theory, creates incentives for more environmentally friendly design.**Extended producer’s responsibility (EPR) is the main feature of the E-waste (Management and Handling) Rules, **2011,** wherein the producer of electrical and electronic equipment has the responsibility of managing such equipment after its ‘end of life’, thus the producer is responsible for their products once the consumer discards them. Under this EPR, producers are also entrusted with the responsibility to finance and organize a system to meet the costs involved in complying with EPR.
The later rules strengthened the Extended producer responsibility provisions but it was introduced for the first time in E-Waste (Management & Handling) Rules, 2011.
